records.name: Ellicott
records.locality: El Paso Co., Colorado; 38° 48' 28" N., 104° 34' 10" W.

holdings.description: A complete individual of ellipsoidal form, which had been used as a pounder by the natives.
An obtusely angular specimen, one side of which has been polished and etched, showing coarse Widmanstätten figures. The original surface has been all scaled off.
A complete individual, the surface of which has scaled off somewhat. A polished and etched surface shows coarse Widmanstätten figures.
A rectangular chiseled and sawed fragment. Shows poor Widmanstätten figures.
A sawed, cuneiform piece showing part of an original surface pit.
An irregular, chiseled fragment.
A sawed slab showing natural surface on one edge. Etched surface shows small, indistinct markings.
Oblong, rectangular, sawed piece.
A complete individual, irregularly meniscus-shaped. Surface pitted and torn. Etched surface shows Widmanstätten figures.
A slab 180 x 123 x 5 to 9 mm. in size. Both sides have been etched and show the typical Widmanstätten figures very perfectly. Three edges show the original surface of the mass.
A polished slab 80 x 75 x 6 mm., one surface etched, showing excellent Widmanstätten figures.

A polished slab 278 x 137 x 7 to 17 mm. in size, an entire section of the mass. Much cohenite is present, frequently inclosing flakes and plates of schreibersite. Three irregularly ellipsoidal nodules are prominent in the mass. They consist of troilite associated with more or less of a relatively soft graphitic (?) substance, and surrounded by a shell of schreibersite, outside of which is a shell of cohenite. The Widmanstätten figures are broad and interrupted, giving the mass an almost granular appearance.
A complete individual; in shape an irregular, elongated, four-sided pyramid.
An irregular chiseled piece showing a torn surface.
A polished slab in which the stony portion exceeds the metallic. The iron shows delicate Widmanstätten figures.
A polished piece in which the iron exceeds the stony matter in amount.

A polished slab 126 x 124 x 4 to 6 mm. in size, showing the original surface of the mass on all edges. The Widmanstätten figures are long and slender, with occasional broad bands. Many of the interspaces show a second, much smaller set of markings. One large nodule of troilite.

An irregular piece, one side of which has been polished and etched. The particles of iron show Widmanstätten figures. The outer surface has been much rusted. The olivine and other stony matter exceed the iron in amount, and the whole seems to be identical with the specimens from Rittersgrün, No. 13/2. They are probably parts of the same fall.
Twenty-two complete individuals, ranging in weight from 1.5 g. to 26.2 g. All show the nickel-white, rounded knobs of iron.
An irregular mass, apparently showing none of the original crust.
holdings.provenance: (Purchased from Miss F. A. M. Hitchcock.)

holdings.description: An irregular mass like the last, but showing a little of the crust.

About half of a spheroidal mass, in which the iron much predominates over the included olivine. Much of the original surface has scaled off, but many of the pits still show. Apparently different from No. 13/20, and may be the pallasite of Imilac.
A very irregular, spongy iron, which has lost its original inclusions, closely resembling the original Pallas iron.
A sharp-pointed, irregular mass of very spongy iron, from which the stony matter has largely decomposed and has fallen out to some extent. Apparently different from No. 13/8.
An irregular, spongy iron, which has lost its original inclusions.
A sub-rectangular individual with grayish-black skin, which has been removed at one spot showing the gray interior with its minute particles of iron.

A complete, rudely wedge-shaped individual preserving its dull black crust nearly entire. The surface is indented with closely-set small pits. The description makes it probable that this is the correct locality for this stone, though none was given on the Spang label.
A fragment from the interior, preserving none of the crust. This is a gray rock with minute metallic (iron) points scattered thickly through it.
A complete, hemispheroidal individual, preserving its thin rusty-black crust. A chipped place shows the light gray interior with its metallic grains.

A complete, wedge-shaped individual. The comparatively thick, dull-black crust is intersected by a network of fine cracks. The interior is gray with small brown spots in it, and with numerous fine veins and particles of iron scattered through it.
Two smaller complete individuals presenting the same characters.

A cuboidal fragment from the interior. Light gray rock with numerous small brown patches. Numerous particles of iron are scattered through the mass, and there is one angular piece (part of a crystal ?) of pyrrhotite.

A nearly complete individual, roughly pyramidal in shape, showing broad shallow surface pits. The thin, dull-black crust has been broken off in spots, revealing the dark gray interior with minute metallic points scattered through it. This is the fall entered as “West Liberty” in the British Museum catalogue of meteorites.
